directions.name: Retune The Hot-Band Steady-State Handoff On The New Grouped-Rows-4 Base
directions.hypothesis: Run `20260420_180146_bf16_gemm_v1_2e4dd24` is now the accepted base and the best custom result at 24.44441605 ms, beating CUTLASS while keeping the winning PTX hot-band microkernel surface intact. The grouped-rows-4 restore already proved that locality was still worth about 5.85 ms versus the regressed round-1 run, and it improved the dominant hot-band kernel itself from 32.97 ms on `da1a5bb` to 32.80 ms while dropping long scoreboard from 8.02% to 7.43%. But the hot-band kernel still dominates the profile and the remaining stall mix is still steady-state handoff shaped: tensor active is only 48.16%, barrier stall is 5.61%, and long scoreboard is still the largest hot-band stall at 7.43%. The best next move is therefore a bounded cp.async wait/commit plus consume-order retime on top of the new grouped-rows-4 base, not another broad structural pivot. That is also the best-measured family history on nearby PTX surfaces: the earlier B-first / consume / barrier retime sequence repeatedly improved similar accepted bases before this newer locality win, so the same bottleneck is still open on the stronger surface.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Steady-state cp.async handoff latency and fragment-consume ordering inside the active 128x128 PTX hot-band loop, which is still leaving tensor issue on the table after the grouped-row locality fix.
directions.risk: Medium. This stays inside the winning hot-band family and targets the dominant remaining stall class, but it is easy to drift into a broader stage rewrite or to undo the new grouped-rows-4 win if the handoff is widened beyond a local retime.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the change does not improve on 24.44441605 ms or if it turns into a wider stage/order rewrite that reopens the closed K32 or broad fixed-main families.

directions.name: Trim PTX Accumulator And Export Live Range On The New Best Surface
directions.hypothesis: The new best run improved locality and scoreboard behavior, but it did not change the occupancy wall: the hot-band kernel is still at 200 registers per thread, `launch__occupancy_limit_registers = 2`, and only 16.60% active warps. That makes register pressure the cleanest materially different follow-up family after the handoff retime. The current PTX microkernel still holds a full `PtxWmmaAccTileSet64x64`, a single-stage export scratch, and the recursive row-pair export path live across the full warp-tile lifetime. If a narrow lifetime trim can shorten accumulator or export residency without changing the winning grouped-rows-4 launch order, it could raise issue headroom on the same dominant hot-band kernel rather than chasing the much smaller secondary kernels.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register pressure and export-side live-state lifetime in the PTX hot-band microkernel, which is capping occupancy before the kernel reaches a memory-throughput limit.
directions.risk: Medium to high. This attacks a real measured wall, but the path is correctness-sensitive and it is easy to increase instruction count or synchronization while trying to shorten live ranges.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers stay at 200 or higher, occupancy does not improve, or tensor activity falls while runtime fails to beat 24.44441605 ms.

directions.name: Probe A Bounded Hot-Band Seam Repivot Around The Grouped-Rows-4 Winner
directions.hypothesis: The grouped-rows-4 restore changed the hot-band traversal enough that the fixed 6400-row seam may no longer be the best split between the dominant PTX kernel and the follow-on peeled path. The latest NCU rows show the peeled and tail kernels still consume about 1.84 ms of profiled GPU time combined, and both secondary kernels carry much higher barrier stall than the hot-band kernel. That keeps a bounded seam retune alive as a tertiary direction: preserve the winning grouped-rows-4 PTX microkernel, then test whether a nearby pivot such as the earlier 6144-row local best reduces the peeled/tail overhead without disturbing the hot-band win. This stays materially different from the first two directions because it changes boundary placement rather than the hot-band inner loop. It stays third because previous seam evidence was still slower than the current surface, so this is only a bounded audit branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Boundary placement between the 128x128 PTX hot-band kernel and the peeled 64x384 row-band launch, especially whether the fixed 6400-row split is leaving avoidable barrier-heavy work in the secondary kernels.
directions.risk: Medium. The change is mechanically narrow, but the known seam family has weaker prior evidence than the hot-band directions and can easily just shift work between kernels without reducing total time.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the repivot only moves time from the hot-band kernel into the peeled or tail kernels, or if it cannot beat 24.44441605 ms with the grouped-rows-4 hot-band path preserved.

notes: Round 3/50 diagnosis anchored to run `20260420_180146_bf16_gemm_v1_2e4dd24`, which is now the accepted base and the first custom result faster than the local CUTLASS baseline. The ranking therefore stays on the winning grouped-rows-4 PTX surface instead of reopening broad structural pivots. Accepted as the primary family: steady-state hot-band handoff / consume retiming, because the dominant kernel still carries 7.43% long scoreboard and 5.61% barrier stall with tensor active only 48.16%. Accepted as the secondary materially different family: register/export lifetime trimming, because the win did not move the 200-register occupancy wall. Deferred to tertiary: a bounded seam repivot, because the peeled+tail kernels are still barrier-heavy but prior seam evidence remains weaker than the hot-band families. Rejected for this round: reopening K32 or broad fixed-main control families, because the latest measured evidence already beat CUTLASS on the current surface and those broader pivots are not the best next tradeoff.
